title: Tender notices - Advanced Contract Award Notice | CanadaBuys

breadcrumbs.title: Request for information or Letter of interest

Minimum solicitation period for ITQs, RFSAs and urgent procurements

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: Advance Contract Award Notice

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: An Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) is a public notice that signals the intent to award a contract for goods, services or construction to a pre-identified business, believed to be the sole business capable of performing the work. This notice allows other businesses the opportunity to signal their interest by submitting a statement of capabilities to demonstrate their ability to meet the set requirements. If no valid statement of capabilities are presented, the contract may be awarded to the pre-identified business.

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: It is crucial to understand that an ACAN is not a competitive process. The ACAN process, even if conducted in accordance with the policy, does not constitute a competitive process for the purposes of the trade agreements and any Canadian International Trade Tribunal (CITT) challenge.

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: For contract approval authorities only, a contract awarded after posting an ACAN for which no valid statement of capabilities is submitted within the notice period is deemed a competitive contract.

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: Objectives of the ACAN process are to:

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: ensure the procurement process is efficient, cost-effective, fair, open and transparent

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: provide businesses with the opportunity to demonstrate, by way of a statement of capabilities, that they are capable of satisfying the requirements set out in the ACAN

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: respect the principles of government contracting by enhancing fairness, access and transparency

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: The conditions for using an ACAN are as follows:

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: It may be used only when there is sufficient justification for not soliciting offers in accordance with the exceptions of the Government Contracts Regulations (GCRs ), and, if applicable, in accordance with the limited tendering reasons set out in the applicable trade agreements

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: The ACAN notice must be published on the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS)

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: The requirement is sufficiently defined so that industry can understand the government’s high level requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: A justification is on file to demonstrate compliance with one of the exceptions of GCRs and if applicable, one of the exclusions or limited tendering reasons of the applicable trade agreements

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: A procurement plan or Contract Plan and Advance Approval (CPAA) as applicable is on the procurement file before posting an ACAN

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: The pre-identified business meets the same criteria to be used for assessment of other businesses who submit statements of capabilities

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: Before publishing an ACAN , you must ensure that:

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: Canada is in a position to legitimately receive and process any submitted statements of capabilities

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: there are no program or policy reasons preventing Canada from accepting such statements of capabilities from another business

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: it is not being used to bypass electronic or other bidding processes when it is clear more than one capable supplier exists that can perform the work

structure_blocks.heading: Advance Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: ACAN s are not phrased in a manner that deters potential submissions of statements of capabilities, such as stating the process is non-competitive when it is not warranted

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: You must ensure Canada is in a position to accept a statement of capabilities before publishing an ACAN .

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: In circumstances where there is no possibility of another business submitting a statement of capabilities or where Canada cannot, for program or policy reasons, accept a statement of capabilities from another business, then the contract should be awarded on a non-competitive basis with transparency achieved through a contract award notice.

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: In such cases, the rationale underlying the decision not to publish an ACAN should be well documented on the procurement file.

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Examples of situations, goods, or services where an ACAN is not to be published include:

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: When disclosing information publicly through an ACAN could compromise security or public interest

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Confirming orders

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Corps of Commissionaires, if a right of first refusal is applicable

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Government-directed procurements such as those under the Munitions Supply Program

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Works of art

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Where, for reasons of extreme urgency brought about by events the entity did not foresee, the goods or services could not be obtained in time by means of open or selective tendering processes

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Tobacco products purchased for inmates by Correctional Service Canada

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Regulatory body determined non-competitive service contracts (for example, National Transportation Agency, Canadian Radio-Television and Telecommunications Commission)

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Consolidated announcements that advertise a program consisting of several non-competitive standing offers/contracts (pharmaceutical and medical supplies are the only products currently eligible for this exclusion)

structure_blocks.heading: Exceptions
structure_blocks.text: When an entity needs to procure consulting services regarding matters of a confidential nature, the disclosure of which could reasonably be expected to compromise government confidences, cause economic disruption or similarly be contrary to the public interest

structure_blocks.heading: Posting period
structure_blocks.text: Posting period

structure_blocks.heading: Posting period
structure_blocks.text: ACAN s are initially posted for a minimum duration of 15 calendar days on GETS . You may extend this period based on the specific requirements and complexity of the procurement. When extending the posting period, you should assess the complexity of the procurement to decide if a longer posting period is necessary to allow adequate time for potential offerors to respond.

structure_blocks.heading: Posting period
structure_blocks.text: You may engage in negotiations with the pre-identified business during the ACAN posting period for efficiency and timeliness; However, you must ensure they are not given an unfair advantage should a successful challenge take place before contract award. You should ensure that the pre-identified business does not begin work or incur costs until an official contract award, preventing financial liabilities should the contract process change following a successful procurement challenge.

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
heading_path: Preparing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: Preparing an Advanced Contract Award Notice

structure_blocks.heading: Preparing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
heading_path: Preparing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: You are responsible for preparing and publishing ACAN s on GETS . To ensure clarity and uniformity, you must include comprehensive details in the ACAN , as prescribed in the mandatory Advance Contract Award Notice template in Forms, templates and guides .

structure_blocks.heading: Amending or re-issuing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
heading_path: Amending or re-issuing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: Amending or re-issuing an Advanced Contract Award Notice

structure_blocks.heading: Amending or re-issuing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
heading_path: Amending or re-issuing an Advanced Contract Award Notice
structure_blocks.text: If new information not specifically set out in the ACAN becomes available or a significant clarification is required, you must provide them equally to all interested parties to ensure fairness, openness and transparency. This may require issuing an amended ACAN with an extended closing date or reissuing a new one.

structure_blocks.heading: Considering a statement of capabilities
structure_blocks.text: Considering a statement of capabilities

structure_blocks.heading: Considering a statement of capabilities
structure_blocks.text: The ACAN process provides businesses with an opportunity to submit a statement of capabilities regarding work identified in an ACAN . Businesses must provide their complete statements of capabilities outlining how they meet the set procurement requirements and evaluation criteria, in writing and within the timeframe specified in the ACAN .

structure_blocks.heading: Late submissions
structure_blocks.text: Late submissions

structure_blocks.heading: Late submissions
structure_blocks.text: Occasionally, you may consider statements of capability received after the specified date but before a contract award. This should be done in consultation with management and legal services.

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Review process

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Following receipt of a statement of capabilities, the review process is as follows:

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Review all statements of capabilities received by the timeframe in accordance with the criteria provided in the ACAN , applying the same standards to the pre-identified business

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: If a statement sufficiently meets the ACAN requirements, inform the business that you will proceed with a competitive process

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Make sure rejected statements of capability undergo a separate review by a higher authority, at least one level above the approval authority to ensure fairness and adherence to procedural standards but no higher than the following:

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Complexity Level 1: Manager

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Complexity Level 2: Director/Regional Director

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Complexity Level 3: Director General/Regional Director General

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Complexity Level 4 and 5: Assistant Deputy Minister, Acquisitions Branch

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Formally notify in writing businesses whose statements of capability are rejected, providing reasons for the rejection

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Ensure requests from businesses to withdraw/cancel their statement of capabilities are documented on file, and should be provided in writing by the business

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Special considerations include:

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: If the procurement is being set-aside under the Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business, evaluate only statements from Indigenous suppliers

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: If the requirement subject to an ACAN is cancelled, you must promptly notify businesses that submitted statements of capabilities

structure_blocks.heading: Review process
structure_blocks.text: Seek additional information to confirm a business' capability to meet the ACAN requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Documenting the procurement file
structure_blocks.text: Documenting the procurement file

structure_blocks.heading: Documenting the procurement file
structure_blocks.text: When proceeding with an ACAN , you are required to meticulously document the procurement file. The documentation should capture all actions and justifications at each phase of the ACAN process to ensure transparency and accountability. This includes rationale for not publishing an ACAN .

structure_blocks.heading: Documenting the procurement file
structure_blocks.text: The documentation ensures transparency and maintains integrity in procurement practices. It supports the non-competitive decision by providing clear, justifiable reasons aligned with policy and operational needs.

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: Key documentation requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: Documentation must include the following justifications, reasons and associated steps, as a minimum:

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: The rationale for the pre-identified business including the relevant exception to soliciting offers under the GCRs and, as applicable, the limited tendering reasons under the trade agreements

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: The actions take to determine if other businesses may have been capable of performing the work. When an industry analysis is conducted that identifies potential businesses capable of fulfilling the procurement requirements, note the following:

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: If only one business is identified and there remains uncertainty about the existence of other capable businesses, an ACAN would be published

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: The justification for choosing a single known business based on this analysis, along with an approved procurement strategy, must be recorded in the procurement file before issuing an ACAN

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: If an exhaustive industry analysis has been completed and there is no doubt that only one business is capable of performing the required work, then a non-competitive process would be more appropriate and an ACAN would not be posted

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: When invoking exception 6(d) of the GCRs for procurements above $25K, the responses to the questions contained in Treasury Board questions for sole source must also accompany the approval document and be on file

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: The assessment criteria and the assessment of the pre-identified business

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: An approved procurement strategy

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: The ACAN notice(s) posted on the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS) and, if applicable, the amended notice

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: The process followed in either accepting or rejecting a statement of capabilities from another business or businesses, including the independent review, if required and the final assessment of the businesses

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: Evidence that all businesses who submitted a statement of capabilities were formally notified of the outcome and were given the reasons why their statement of capabilities was rejected, if requested

structure_blocks.heading: Key documentation requirements
structure_blocks.text: Details of any resulting competitive process, if conducted

raw_text:
Advance Contract Award Notice

An Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) is a public notice that signals the intent to award a contract for goods, services or construction to a pre-identified business, believed to be the sole business capable of performing the work. This notice allows other businesses the opportunity to signal their interest by submitting a statement of capabilities to demonstrate their ability to meet the set requirements. If no valid statement of capabilities are presented, the contract may be awarded to the pre-identified business.

It is crucial to understand that an ACAN is not a competitive process. The ACAN process, even if conducted in accordance with the policy, does not constitute a competitive process for the purposes of the trade agreements and any Canadian International Trade Tribunal (CITT) challenge.

For contract approval authorities only, a contract awarded after posting an ACAN for which no valid statement of capabilities is submitted within the notice period is deemed a competitive contract.

Objectives of the ACAN process are to:

ensure the procurement process is efficient, cost-effective, fair, open and transparent

provide businesses with the opportunity to demonstrate, by way of a statement of capabilities, that they are capable of satisfying the requirements set out in the ACAN

respect the principles of government contracting by enhancing fairness, access and transparency

The conditions for using an ACAN are as follows:

It may be used only when there is sufficient justification for not soliciting offers in accordance with the exceptions of the Government Contracts Regulations (GCRs ), and, if applicable, in accordance with the limited tendering reasons set out in the applicable trade agreements

The ACAN notice must be published on the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS)

The requirement is sufficiently defined so that industry can understand the government’s high level requirements

A justification is on file to demonstrate compliance with one of the exceptions of GCRs and if applicable, one of the exclusions or limited tendering reasons of the applicable trade agreements

A procurement plan or Contract Plan and Advance Approval (CPAA) as applicable is on the procurement file before posting an ACAN

The pre-identified business meets the same criteria to be used for assessment of other businesses who submit statements of capabilities

Before publishing an ACAN , you must ensure that:

Canada is in a position to legitimately receive and process any submitted statements of capabilities

there are no program or policy reasons preventing Canada from accepting such statements of capabilities from another business

it is not being used to bypass electronic or other bidding processes when it is clear more than one capable supplier exists that can perform the work

ACAN s are not phrased in a manner that deters potential submissions of statements of capabilities, such as stating the process is non-competitive when it is not warranted

Exceptions

You must ensure Canada is in a position to accept a statement of capabilities before publishing an ACAN .

In circumstances where there is no possibility of another business submitting a statement of capabilities or where Canada cannot, for program or policy reasons, accept a statement of capabilities from another business, then the contract should be awarded on a non-competitive basis with transparency achieved through a contract award notice.

In such cases, the rationale underlying the decision not to publish an ACAN should be well documented on the procurement file.

Examples of situations, goods, or services where an ACAN is not to be published include:

When disclosing information publicly through an ACAN could compromise security or public interest

Confirming orders

Corps of Commissionaires, if a right of first refusal is applicable

Government-directed procurements such as those under the Munitions Supply Program

Works of art

Where, for reasons of extreme urgency brought about by events the entity did not foresee, the goods or services could not be obtained in time by means of open or selective tendering processes

Tobacco products purchased for inmates by Correctional Service Canada

Regulatory body determined non-competitive service contracts (for example, National Transportation Agency, Canadian Radio-Television and Telecommunications Commission)

Consolidated announcements that advertise a program consisting of several non-competitive standing offers/contracts (pharmaceutical and medical supplies are the only products currently eligible for this exclusion)

When an entity needs to procure consulting services regarding matters of a confidential nature, the disclosure of which could reasonably be expected to compromise government confidences, cause economic disruption or similarly be contrary to the public interest

Posting period

ACAN s are initially posted for a minimum duration of 15 calendar days on GETS . You may extend this period based on the specific requirements and complexity of the procurement. When extending the posting period, you should assess the complexity of the procurement to decide if a longer posting period is necessary to allow adequate time for potential offerors to respond.

You may engage in negotiations with the pre-identified business during the ACAN posting period for efficiency and timeliness; However, you must ensure they are not given an unfair advantage should a successful challenge take place before contract award. You should ensure that the pre-identified business does not begin work or incur costs until an official contract award, preventing financial liabilities should the contract process change following a successful procurement challenge.

Preparing an Advanced Contract Award Notice

You are responsible for preparing and publishing ACAN s on GETS . To ensure clarity and uniformity, you must include comprehensive details in the ACAN , as prescribed in the mandatory Advance Contract Award Notice template in Forms, templates and guides .

Amending or re-issuing an Advanced Contract Award Notice

If new information not specifically set out in the ACAN becomes available or a significant clarification is required, you must provide them equally to all interested parties to ensure fairness, openness and transparency. This may require issuing an amended ACAN with an extended closing date or reissuing a new one.

Considering a statement of capabilities

The ACAN process provides businesses with an opportunity to submit a statement of capabilities regarding work identified in an ACAN . Businesses must provide their complete statements of capabilities outlining how they meet the set procurement requirements and evaluation criteria, in writing and within the timeframe specified in the ACAN .

Late submissions

Occasionally, you may consider statements of capability received after the specified date but before a contract award. This should be done in consultation with management and legal services.

Review process

Following receipt of a statement of capabilities, the review process is as follows:

Review all statements of capabilities received by the timeframe in accordance with the criteria provided in the ACAN , applying the same standards to the pre-identified business

If a statement sufficiently meets the ACAN requirements, inform the business that you will proceed with a competitive process

Make sure rejected statements of capability undergo a separate review by a higher authority, at least one level above the approval authority to ensure fairness and adherence to procedural standards but no higher than the following:

Complexity Level 1: Manager

Complexity Level 2: Director/Regional Director

Complexity Level 3: Director General/Regional Director General

Complexity Level 4 and 5: Assistant Deputy Minister, Acquisitions Branch

Formally notify in writing businesses whose statements of capability are rejected, providing reasons for the rejection

Ensure requests from businesses to withdraw/cancel their statement of capabilities are documented on file, and should be provided in writing by the business

Special considerations include:

If the procurement is being set-aside under the Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business, evaluate only statements from Indigenous suppliers

If the requirement subject to an ACAN is cancelled, you must promptly notify businesses that submitted statements of capabilities

Seek additional information to confirm a business' capability to meet the ACAN requirements

Documenting the procurement file

When proceeding with an ACAN , you are required to meticulously document the procurement file. The documentation should capture all actions and justifications at each phase of the ACAN process to ensure transparency and accountability. This includes rationale for not publishing an ACAN .

The documentation ensures transparency and maintains integrity in procurement practices. It supports the non-competitive decision by providing clear, justifiable reasons aligned with policy and operational needs.

Key documentation requirements

Documentation must include the following justifications, reasons and associated steps, as a minimum:

The rationale for the pre-identified business including the relevant exception to soliciting offers under the GCRs and, as applicable, the limited tendering reasons under the trade agreements

The actions take to determine if other businesses may have been capable of performing the work. When an industry analysis is conducted that identifies potential businesses capable of fulfilling the procurement requirements, note the following:

If only one business is identified and there remains uncertainty about the existence of other capable businesses, an ACAN would be published

The justification for choosing a single known business based on this analysis, along with an approved procurement strategy, must be recorded in the procurement file before issuing an ACAN

If an exhaustive industry analysis has been completed and there is no doubt that only one business is capable of performing the required work, then a non-competitive process would be more appropriate and an ACAN would not be posted

When invoking exception 6(d) of the GCRs for procurements above $25K, the responses to the questions contained in Treasury Board questions for sole source must also accompany the approval document and be on file

The assessment criteria and the assessment of the pre-identified business

An approved procurement strategy

The ACAN notice(s) posted on the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS) and, if applicable, the amended notice

The process followed in either accepting or rejecting a statement of capabilities from another business or businesses, including the independent review, if required and the final assessment of the businesses

Evidence that all businesses who submitted a statement of capabilities were formally notified of the outcome and were given the reasons why their statement of capabilities was rejected, if requested

Details of any resulting competitive process, if conducted

Advance Contract Award Notice

An Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) is a public notice that signals the intent to award a contract for goods, services or construction to a pre-identified business, believed to be the sole business capable of performing the work. This notice allows other businesses the opportunity to signal their interest by submitting a statement of capabilities to demonstrate their ability to meet the set requirements. If no valid statement of capabilities are presented, the contract may be awarded to the pre-identified business.

It is crucial to understand that an ACAN is not a competitive process. The ACAN process, even if conducted in accordance with the policy, does not constitute a competitive process for the purposes of the trade agreements and any Canadian International Trade Tribunal (CITT) challenge.

Objectives of the ACAN process are to:

ensure the procurement process is efficient, cost-effective, fair, open and transparent

Works of art

Where, for reasons of extreme urgency brought about by events the entity did not foresee, the goods or services could not be obtained in time by means of open or selective tendering processes

ACAN s are initially posted for a minimum duration of 15 calendar days on GETS . You may extend this period based on the specific requirements and complexity of the procurement. When extending the posting period, you should assess the complexity of the procurement to decide if a longer posting period is necessary to allow adequate time for potential offerors to respond.

You should ensure that the pre-identified business does not begin work or incur costs until an official contract award, preventing financial liabilities should the contract process change following a successful procurement challenge.

You are responsible for preparing and publishing ACAN s on GETS . To ensure clarity and uniformity, you must include comprehensive details in the ACAN , as prescribed in the mandatory Advance Contract Award Notice template in Forms, templates and guides .

Amending or re-issuing an Advanced Contract Award Notice

If new information not specifically set out in the ACAN becomes available or a significant clarification is required, you must provide them equally to all interested parties to ensure fairness, openness and transparency. This may require issuing an amended ACAN with an extended closing date or reissuing a new one.

Considering a statement of capabilities

The ACAN process provides businesses with an opportunity to submit a statement of capabilities regarding work identified in an ACAN . Late submissions

Occasionally, you may consider statements of capability received after the specified date but before a contract award. This should be done in consultation with management and legal services.

Review process

Following receipt of a statement of capabilities, the review process is as follows:

Review all statements of capabilities received by the timeframe in accordance with the criteria provided in the ACAN , applying the same standards to the pre-identified business

If a statement sufficiently meets the ACAN requirements, inform the business that you will proceed with a competitive process

Make sure rejected statements of capability undergo a separate review by a higher authority, at least one level above the approval authority to ensure fairness and adherence to procedural standards but no higher than the following:

Complexity Level 1: Manager

Complexity Level 2: Director/Regional Director

Complexity Level 3: Director General/Regional Director General

Complexity Level 4 and 5: Assistant Deputy Minister, Acquisitions Branch

Formally notify in writing businesses whose statements of capability are rejected, providing reasons for the rejection

Ensure requests from businesses to withdraw/cancel their statement of capabilities are documented on file, and should be provided in writing by the business

Special considerations include:

If the procurement is being set-aside under the Procurement Strategy for Indigenous Business, evaluate only statements from Indigenous suppliers

If the requirement subject to an ACAN is cancelled, you must promptly notify businesses that submitted statements of capabilities

Seek additional information to confirm a business' capability to meet the ACAN requirements

Documenting the procurement file

When proceeding with an ACAN , you are required to meticulously document the procurement file. The documentation should capture all actions and justifications at each phase of the ACAN process to ensure transparency and accountability. This includes rationale for not publishing an ACAN .

The documentation ensures transparency and maintains integrity in procurement practices. It supports the non-competitive decision by providing clear, justifiable reasons aligned with policy and operational needs.

Documentation must include the following justifications, reasons and associated steps, as a minimum:

The rationale for the pre-identified business including the relevant exception to soliciting offers under the GCRs and, as applicable, the limited tendering reasons under the trade agreements

The actions take to determine if other businesses may have been capable of performing the work. When an industry analysis is conducted that identifies potential businesses capable of fulfilling the procurement requirements, note the following:

If only one business is identified and there remains uncertainty about the existence of other capable businesses, an ACAN would be published

The justification for choosing a single known business based on this analysis, along with an approved procurement strategy, must be recorded in the procurement file before issuing an ACAN

If an exhaustive industry analysis has been completed and there is no doubt that only one business is capable of performing the required work, then a non-competitive process would be more appropriate and an ACAN would not be posted

When invoking exception 6(d) of the GCRs for procurements above $25K, the responses to the questions contained in Treasury Board questions for sole source must also accompany the approval document and be on file

The assessment criteria and the assessment of the pre-identified business

An approved procurement strategy

The ACAN notice(s) posted on the Government Electronic Tendering Service (GETS) and, if applicable, the amended notice

The process followed in either accepting or rejecting a statement of capabilities from another business or businesses, including the independent review, if required and the final assessment of the businesses

Evidence that all businesses who submitted a statement of capabilities were formally notified of the outcome and were given the reasons why their statement of capabilities was rejected, if requested

Details of any resulting competitive process, if conducted
